data.title: Australia Shares Shaping for Flat OpenMarket Talk
data.description: Australia's S&P/ASX 200 is poised for a flat open after U.S. stocks edged lower amid continued confusion about the Trump administration's tariff plans. ASX futures are barely moved ahead of Wednesday's session, suggesting that investors in Australia are also waiting for clarity on tariffs. Ahead of the open, mining giant Rio Tinto said it expects iron-ore shipments from its Australian mining operations to be at the lower end of annual guidance. Mineral-sands miner Iluka reported lower 1Q revenue on softer prices. In the U.S., the DJIA lost 0.4%, the S&P 500 fell 0.2%, and the Nasdaq Composite declined by less than 0.1%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: KKR to acquire German IT firm Datagroup for about $500 million
data.description:
Frankfurt-listed IT services provider Datagroup said on Tuesday that it had entered into an all-cash acquisition agreement with U.S.-based private equity firm KKR , valuing the company at about 450 million euros ($508.32 million).

KKR would pay 54 euros for each Datagroup share held, representing a 33% premium on the company's last closing price of 40.75 euros.

Datagroup will subsequently delist from the exchange once the purchase is settled. The company expects the deal to close in the third quarter of 2025, it said in a statement.

Datagroup, employing about 3,700 staff across Germany, projected in March that its annual revenue would increase to between 545 million euros and 565 million euros this year.

The deal between KKR and Datagroup was first reported by Bloomberg News.

($1 = 0.8853 euros)

data.title: US judge blocks Trump's freeze on climate, infrastructure grants
data.description:
A U.S. judge blocked President Donald Trump 's administration on Tuesday from freezing billions of dollars in grants Congress authorized under two signature climate investment and infrastructure laws of his Democratic predecessor, former President Joe Biden.

U.S. District Judge Mary McElroy in Providence, Rhode Island, issued an injunction at the behest of environmental groups who argued the Trump administration was unlawfully freezing already-awarded funding for projects to combat climate change, reduce pollution and modernize U.S. infrastructure.

That funding had been authorized by Congress pursuant to Biden's $1-trillion bill known as the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act of 2021 and his signature 2022 climate investment law, the Inflation Reduction Act.

The funding was frozen after Trump on his first day back in office on January 20 signed an executive order directing agencies to pause funding approved under those two laws, pending a review to ensure the spending supported his policies.

Following that order, the Environmental Protection Agency and the U.S. Departments of Agriculture, Energy, Interior, and Housing and Urban Development each paused the distribution of grant funding.

In a lawsuit filed on March 13, the Woonasquatucket River Watershed Council, National Council of Nonprofits, Eastern Rhode Island Conservation District and Green Infrastructure Center argued the agencies lacked authority to unilaterally withhold already-awarded congressionally-authorized funds.

They said the agencies froze the money despite earlier rulings by another judge in Rhode Island, who at the urging of a group of Democratic-led states, blocked the administration from carrying out a sweeping, blanket freeze on $3 trillion in federal grants, loans and other financial assistance.

The Trump administration countered it was entitled to temporarily pause funding for those currently awarded grants to decide whether to redirect funding elsewhere, and that the Rhode Island judge lacked jurisdiction to hear the case.

The U.S. Department of Justice said its position was bolstered after the U.S. Supreme Court in a 5-4 ruling on April 4 cleared the way for the administration to terminate millions of dollars in teacher training grants as part of Trump's crackdown on diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives.

data.title: Has the VIX peaked? 'Acute phase' of selloff in stocks may be done, says Bespoke.
data.description:
By Christine Idzelis

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in an interview this week that he thought the VIX has 'likely peaked'

Stock-market volatility has seen outsized swings up and down this month, as investors contend with uncertain negotiations around the "reciprocal" tariffs announced by White House on April 2.

"We think it's safe to say that the acute phase of the selloff is over," said Bespoke Investment Group, in a note after the U.S. stock market closed Tuesday. The firm cited a third straight day of declines for the Cboe Volatility Index - Wall Street's so-called fear gauge that trades under the ticker symbol VIX - and noted that it dipped below 30 during Tuesday's trading session.

The VIX VIX closed Tuesday at 30.12, falling 2.5%, according to FactSet data. That level is above the gauge's long-term average of around 20 but well below its surge last week.

As investors look for clarity on tariffs, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in an interview Monday with Bloomberg Television that "the market can take great comfort" that the White House is going to "run a robust process." He pointed to the Cboe Volatility Index, suggesting that by that measure, the market may have seen peak uncertainty.

"If we measure uncertainty by the VIX," said Bessent, then "I think the VIX spiked and has likely peaked."

The VIX soared after President Donald Trump announced on April 2 sweeping tariffs that upset the global order of trade, with investors now watching for developments in negotiations between the White House and its trading partners. Investors had appeared unnerved by the large size and scope of the tariffs Trump rolled out. A big slump in U.S. stocks ensued amid fears that large levies imposed on goods imported from other countries risked causing a recession.

But equities bounced last week, after Trump announced a 90-day pause on "reciprocal" tariffs for countries outside China.

"It's been a wild ride in the markets, and nowhere have the extremes been more on display than in the CBOE Volatility Index," said Bespoke, in a separate note emailed Tuesday afternoon before the stock market closed.

"The VIX experienced its second-largest five-day increase on record last Tuesday," and then appeared "on pace for its second-largest five-day decline on record," Bespoke said. Based on the VIX at one point trading below 29 during Tuesday's session, Bespoke calculated the gauge had seen a five-day decline of 23.6 points, according to the firm's note.

Read: White House hints at some trade-deal progress - minus China. Here's how Trump is pressing Xi.

The U.S. stock market ended lower Tuesday, with the S&P 500 SPX declining 0.2%, the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA falling 0.4% and the technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite COMP slipping less than 0.1%, according to Dow Jones Market Data. All three indexes snapped back-to-back gains.

The S&P 500 is down more than 8% so far this year, with Big Tech stocks particularly hard hit, according to FactSet data. For example, shares of Tesla Inc. (TSLA) have tumbled around 37% year to date, while Apple Inc. (AAPL) has plunged around 19% this year through Tuesday.

Check out: Trump gave tech companies a break from tariffs. Here's how long that could last.

-Christine Idzelis

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Iluka 1Q Output Rises On-Quarter; Sales, Revenue Fall as Prices Drop
data.description:
(22:27 GMT) *Iluka: 1Q Zircon, Rutile and Synthetic Rutile Production 131kt
(22:27 GMT) *Iluka: 1Q Zircon, Rutile and Synthetic Rutile Sales 116kt
(22:28 GMT) *Iluka: 1Q Weighted Average Zircon Sand Price US$1,698/t
(22:30 GMT) *Iluka: Contracted 46kt of Zircon Sand Sales and 32kt of Zircon-in-Concentrate to Date in 2Q
(22:30 GMT) *Iluka: 2Q Zircon Sand Sales Prices Are in Line with 1Q
(22:31 GMT) *Iluka: Construction Work Progressing Well at Balranald Project
(22:32 GMT) *Iluka: 1Q Zircon, Rutile and Synthetic Rutile Production +51% on Year
(22:32 GMT) *Iluka: 1Q Zircon, Rutile and Synthetic Rutile Sales +10% on Year
(22:33 GMT) *Iluka: Heightened Uncertainty in Global Economy Due to Tariffs, Overall Impact Unpredictable
(22:34 GMT) *Iluka: Titanium Dioxide Feedstocks, Rare Earths Are Exempt from U.S. Tariffs
(22:34 GMT) *Iluka: China Has Added Medium and Heavy Rare Earths to Export Control List
(22:36 GMT) *Iluka: Zircon Not Exempt from U.S. Tariffs, But Zircon Sales to U.S. Were Just 6% of 2024 Sales
(22:36 GMT) *Iluka: All Major Equipment Packages Have Been Awarded at Eneabba Rare Earths Refinery
(23:31 GMT) Iluka 1Q Output Rises On-Quarter; Sales, Revenue Fall as Prices Drop

By Rhiannon Hoyle

Iluka Resources produced more of its core mineral sands products quarter over quarter, but said that sales and revenue fell as market prices weakened.

The Australian miner on Wednesday reported first-quarter output of zircon, rutile and synthetic rutile totaling 130,700 metric tons, up 1.0% on the final quarter of 2024.

Yet sales fell by nearly 15%, to 116,200 tons, while revenue was about 10% lower, at 260 million Australian dollars (US$165 million).

Iluka said the price it received for its zircon dropped to US$1,698 a ton, from US$1,819 a ton the quarter immediately prior.

"This is a modest price decrease despite larger price reductions by major competitors," the company said.

The prices Iluka fetched for its rutile and synthetic rutile products fell by 6.8% and 4.0%, respectively, versus the final quarter of last year.

Iluka is the world's largest producer of zircon, a mineral used to produce ceramic tiles. It is also a top supplier of rutile, primarily used as a whitening pigment in paints, plastics and paper.

Write to Rhiannon Hoyle at rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com

data.title: UMC Electronics Ordered to Pay 463 Million Pesos After Tax Appeal Ruling
data.description:
UMC Electronics said its subsidiary, UMC Electronics Mexico, was ordered by Mexican tax authorities to pay 463 million pesos (approximately 3.3 billion yen) following an appeal ruling, according to a Tuesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The decision reduced the original tax assessment of 516 million pesos.

The company has 30 business days to file a lawsuit in the Tax Court if it intends to contest the ruling.

data.title: Nihon Chouzai Addresses Privatization Bidding Process Reports
data.description:
Nihon Chouzai responded to news reports regarding a potential privatization, confirming that while the company is exploring strategic options to enhance corporate value, no decisions have been made, according to a Tuesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The company clarified that it has not made any announcements related to the bidding process, as reported by Mergermarket.

data.title: Trump says he plans stipends as part of self-deportation program
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said in an interview aired on Tuesday that he planned to roll out a new program offering money for immigrants in the country illegally to leave voluntarily.

Trump said such a "self-deportation program" would include some financial assistance and the prospect of re-entering the country later legally.

"We're going to give them a stipend," Trump told Fox Noticias' Rachel Campos-Duffy, according to an official transcript of an interview taped on Monday.

"We're going to give them some money and a plane ticket, and then we're going to work with them. If they're good, if we want them back in, we're going to work with them to get them back in as quickly as we can."

A White House spokesman said he had nothing to add to Trump's comments.

Trump, a Republican, has vowed to deport record numbers of migrants in the U.S. illegally and has tested the bounds of U.S. law to increase arrests and deportations.

His administration has also pushed immigrants to assist in their own expulsion. Immigrants are being asked to signal their "intent to depart" using a U.S. Customs and Border Protection app called CBP Home.

data.title: US Futures Decline as Nvidia Drags
data.description:
U.S. stock futures fell early Wednesday, pressured by a sharp drop in Nvidia shares during after-hours trading.

The chipmaker sank around 6% after revealing a $5.5 billion quarterly charge tied to export restrictions on its H20 graphics processing units to China and other countries.

Investors are also bracing for a wave of corporate earnings set to be released later in the day, including reports from Abbott, U.S. Bancorp, and Travelers.

Meanwhile, March retail sales data is due, offering a potential glimpse into how growing tariff concerns are shaping consumer behavior.

On Tuesday, major indexes closed lower amid lingering trade-related uncertainty.

The Dow slipped 0.38%, the S&P 500 edged down 0.17%, and the Nasdaq Composite lost 0.05%.

Market participants remain focused on escalating tensions between the U.S. and China, as the Commerce Department opened an investigation into semiconductor and pharmaceutical imports—raising the prospect of further tariffs.

data.title: Trump Picks IRS Critic as Agency's Acting Leader
data.description:
By Richard Rubin and C. Ryan Barber

WASHINGTON — President Trump named Gary Shapley — the IRS criminal investigator who criticized the government for its handling of Hunter Biden's tax filings — as the tax agency's acting commissioner, according to a memo reviewed by The Wall Street Journal.

Shapley is the Internal Revenue Service's fourth leader in less than four months as the Trump administration rapidly shrinks the agency. During the Biden administration, Shapley rose to prominence as an IRS supervisory criminal agent who came forward to Congress and contended that the Justice Department had slow-walked its tax investigation into Hunter Biden.

Shapley replaces Melanie Krause, who had been acting commissioner since late February and announced her departure after the Treasury Department and the Department of Homeland Security signed a data-sharing agreement aimed at helping the government find some immigrants suspected of crimes. Krause replaced Douglas O'Donnell, a longtime agency employee who retired in February. O'Donnell replaced Danny Werfel, who resigned on Inauguration Day.

Trump's pick to run the IRS permanently is Billy Long, a former Republican congressman from Missouri. He hasn't yet had a confirmation hearing in the Senate.

Shapley's ascension came on the annual individual income-tax filing deadline, in the middle of the year's busiest moment for the IRS.

The White House didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Write to Richard Rubin at richard.rubin@wsj.com and C. Ryan Barber at ryan.barber@wsj.com

data.title: Japan to forgo submitting extra budget to current Diet session, Nikkei reports
data.description:
Japan's government will forgo submitting a supplementary budget during the current parliament session, the Nikkei newspaper reported on Wednesday, citing a senior ruling party official.

Instead, the government will use budget reserves to re-introduce subsidies aimed at curbing utility bills, the Nikkei said.

Some ruling coalition lawmakers have called for compiling a large-scale spending package to cushion the economic blow from rising cost of living and U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff policy.

Such a package would have required the government to submit a supplementary budget to the current parliament session that runs through June.

data.title: Gambian man convicted by US jury for role in torture program
data.description:
A man who was accused of being part of a Gambian armed unit run by former dictator Yahya Jammeh has been convicted in the United States for torture, the Justice Department said on Tuesday.

A Colorado jury convicted the Gambian national, Michael Sang Correa, for his participation in the torture of numerous victims in The Gambia in 2006, including through beating and flesh burning, because of the victims' purported involvement in a coup plot against the then-president, the Justice Department added.

The case marked the first known time that someone faced criminal prosecution over the role of the feared armed group known as "the Junglers" in Gambia's police state during the rule of Jammeh, who seized power in 1994 and foiled several attempts to overthrow him before he lost an election in late 2016.

Correa was arrested in 2020 under a law which makes it a crime for anyone in the United States to commit torture abroad.

Jammeh denied torture during his rule.

The Junglers were a secretive offshoot of the Gambian army that took orders from Jammeh. Rights groups and former victims say they carried out a brutal form of justice that worsened after the failed coup.

Suspected coup plotters and other outspoken opponents of Jammeh were taken to the National Intelligence Agency near one of the capital Banjul's white sand beaches, according to victims who spoke to Reuters.

Some found themselves in a torture chamber where they were shocked with electricity, beaten, burned with acid and smothered with plastic bags, they said.

Correa faces a maximum penalty of 20 years in prison for each of the five torture counts and the count of conspiracy to commit torture, the Justice Department said.

data.title: Air New Zealand Sees Up to NZ$190 Million in Pre-Tax Earnings in Fiscal Year 2025
data.description:
Air New Zealand expects full fiscal year earnings before tax to be within the range of NZ$150 million to NZ$190 million, according to a Wednesday filing with the Australian and New Zealand bourses.

This includes an estimated NZ$35 million to NZ$40 million in compensation related to the maintenance of grounded aircraft for the fiscal second half. This estimate is "substantially lower" than the NZ$94 million recognized in the fiscal first half, Air New Zealand said.

It added that it remains in talks with engine manufacturers about "appropriate levels" of compensation and "accurate timeframes" for returning the unserviceable engines.

"These discussions continue to be complicated, but Air New Zealand is exploring all possible avenues to ensure a fair outcome," the company said in its filing.

Defendants' statements included, among other things, confidence in Geron's ability to capitalize on the purportedly significant unmet need for the drug and to execute on its commercial plan to target first-line ESA ineligible patients, while continually minimizing the risks associated with the burden of the weekly monitoring requirement for Rytelo and the impacts of seasonality and existing competition on the drug's sales.<br/><br/>On February 26, 2025, Geron announced its financial results for the fourth quarter of fiscal 2024, disclosing that Rytelo's growth had flattened over the preceding months. The Company attributed the diminished growth on seasonality, competition, lack of awareness for Rytelo, and the burden of the monitoring requirement necessary for the drug treatment.<br/><br/>Following this news, the price of Geron's common stock declined dramatically. data.title: ChartWatch ASX Scans: Gold stocks still topping the list, watching AZY, CMM, DEG, EVN, GMD, GOR, NST & PRU
data.description:
Key points:
  • Interesting uptrends in today's Scans: Almonty Industries (AII), Alkane Resources (ALK), AMA (AMA), Eagers Automotive (APE), Antipa Minerals (AZY), De Grey Mining (DEG), Genesis Minerals (GMD), Gold Road (GOR), Northern Star (NST), Turaco Gold (TCG).
  • Interesting downtrends in today's Scans: Appen (APX), Coronado Resources (CRN), Flight Centre Travel Group (FLT), Helloworld (HLO), HMC (HMC), Humm (HUM), Lendlease (LLC), NRW (NWH), Skycity (SKC), Treasury Wine Estates (TWE), Tyro Payments (TYR).

Welcome to my ChartWatch Daily ASX Scans series. Here I present scan lists based on my trend following technical analysis methodology. My goal is to alert you to the best uptrends and downtrends on the ASX.

Gold stocks continue to feature heavily in the Uptrends Scan Lists – not a surprise given they've been some of the most consistent and best performing stocks during this correction and for the better part of the last 12 months.

Of course, the big question is: How much longer can this continue? 🔮

The first place to look would be the gold price – as it's fair to say gold stocks' prosperies are directly tied to the price of the commodity they produce!

Gold Futures (Front month, back-adjusted) COMEX

All clear here as far as I'm concerned – or as far as my trend following model is concerned. The gold chart is a picture of excess demand: Well-established short and long term uptrends, trend ribbons that are supporting the price action, rising peaks (not rising troughs – thanks President Trump!), and a predominance of demand-side candles.

Many of the Feature charts you'll see below share the same characteristics. Like gold, I won't know the top is in on them until after the fact – until the demand-supply dynamics shift to delver one-by-one the opposite of the demand-side traits I've listed above.

But I'll worry about that when it happens. Such is the philosophy of trend following! 🧘

Feel free to get your favourite AI to convert the tables below into lists you can upload to your favourite trading platform like TradingView. Then you'll be able to skip from chart to chart and quickly and easily see the best uptrends and downtrends on the ASX.

Some investors prefer to buy those stocks in strong uptrends, and avoid, sell, or short sell those stocks in strong downtrends – but how you use the lists is really up to you!

Note, many stocks in both lists have appeared there many times before. As long as they keep meeting my criteria – they'll keep appearing. But note, there won't be any notifications when they don't, so you'll have to do your own research on when a particular trend changes!

data.title: Magnitude 6.4 earthquake strikes Afghanistan, EMSC says
data.description:
An earthquake of magnitude 6.4 struck Afghanistan on Wednesday, the European-Mediterranean Seismological Centre (EMSC) said.

The quake was at a depth of 121 km (75 miles), EMSC said.

data.title: GOV. MAURA HEALEY (D), MASSACHUSETTS, IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 15, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

GOV. MAURA HEALEY (D), MASSACHUSETTS

GOV. MAURA HEALEY (D), MASSACHUSETTS, IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

GOV. MAURA HEALEY (D), MASSACHUSETTS, IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC

APRIL 15, 2025

SPEAKERS:

GOV. MAURA HEALEY (D), MASSACHUSETTS

ARI MELBER, HOST: We're joined by Massachusetts Democratic Governor Maura Healey. She's praised Harvard in the story we've been covering, saying that they're standing against the Trump administration's brazen attempt to bully schools and weaponize DoJ under the false pretext of civil rights.

Thank you for joining me. What is important about what Harvard is doing?

GOV. MAURA HEALEY (D), MASSACHUSETTS: Harvard's saying enough is enough. And let me be clear, Ari, I was a civil rights lawyer, I was attorney general, there is no place for antisemitism in society, on our college campuses, or anywhere else. And there's no question that Jewish students have been treated poorly on campuses around this country. It's also the case that colleges need to do more. And, in fact, Harvard has done more. And they've said that there is more work for them to do, and they're doing that.

But that's not what is going on here, Ari. And I think this is the point that people need to really understand, that this is really just another attempt by Donald Trump to silence those who disagree with him, to bully, in this case, our colleges and universities in the same way he's bullied and tried to silence law firms and corporations and everyday people, in fact. And that is really, really bad. It's bad for who we are as a country. It's bad for the marketplace of ideas. It's bad and it needs to be stopped.

Today, for example, I was out visiting one of the — one of the great universities here, our medical school here at University of Massachusetts. And last week I was at a Harvard teaching hospital. And these are places, Ari, that rely on that federal funding for their scientists and their researchers. They're using that funding to cure cancer, to cure Alzheimer's.

MELBER: Yeah.

HEALEY: To come up with life-saving measures. And I think people need to understand the economic impact of all of this, and also what it means for everyday lives of Americans around this country and, frankly, around the world.

MELBER: Yeah, you mentioned that, and — and there's so many layers to it because, first, there's the anti-free speech crackdown, the abuse of government power, what I mentioned was very anti-conservative, sort of big government, you know, in your classroom and in independent and private institutions. Second, people think a lot of things when they think Harvard, right? It might be a bit of a Rorschach test for some, but, boy, are they behind a lot of life-saving research.

They write in the statement, which I think you were partially alluding to, "new frontiers beckon" with life-changing advances based on their research, treatments for Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, diabetes, breakthroughs in AI. "For the government," that's referring to the Trump administration, "to retreat from these partnerships risk not only the health and well-being of millions, but economic security and vitality of our nation," from the Harvard Garber statement there.

And it's really true that the administration doesn't care, and I guess doesn't pretend to care about that fallout, because they want to make a point about going after what they see as political opponents.

HEALEY: Yeah, I couldn't agree more. And — and, you know, what people need to understand, this is bigger than Harvard. I mean, Harvard is a wonderful institution, has contributed so many things, when you think about advancements in health, advancements in science, all the support it provides on everything from cybersecurity to our defense industry, right? It's also the case that there are a lot of colleges and universities around the country who are doing this, who are also having their funding cut. This makes no sense.

You know, I am about America first. I want to put this country first. But I can tell you right now, Ari, because of what he's done to Harvard, because of what Trump is doing to other universities, China is recruiting all of our scientists and researchers away. He's giving away intellectual assets, OK? He's taking away the people who are doing the research right now and are on the brink of discoveries to cure cancer and to end disease, to do things that will change the world in positive ways as we know it.

It just makes no sense to me. So not only is it an attack on our colleges and universities, it's also an attack on this country, on our economy, on who we are. We're a leader in innovation, you know, and I want us to be just that. But whether you're talking about Harvard University, whether you're talking about the University of Alabama or University of Georgia, this stuff is happening in real time right now.

I was on a campus today, Ari, where I learned that faculty members were — are being let go. They've had to cancel, rescind offers for students who are engaged in postdoc and scientific research and study programs because this funding is going away.

MELBER: Yeah.

HEALEY: And, you know, it just kills me having — knowing the consequences of this. So, look, it's an abuse of power. It smacks of the authoritarian regimes of past when you talk about silencing law firms, companies, colleges and universities, when you talk about disappearing people from our streets, including foreign students here on work visas. And it also has really significant economic consequences for this country.

If you truly believe in America first and you want to bring — and re-shore manufacturing here, you know what you shouldn't be doing? Sending our researchers and our scientists and the next generation of entrepreneurs overseas, which is what Donald Trump is doing by cutting funding to our colleges and universities.

MELBER: Hm. You're speaking very clearly. Obviously, it's something you know a lot about, and you're the top official, chief executive and top Democrat in the state of Massachusetts. So getting your view on this and what is honestly a sad intersection of politics and Harvard, because it's the government, the Trump administration making it political. It didn't have to be this way. And now — but you know a lot about both. So I do appreciate you coming on "The Beat" on this story tonight, Governor Healey, thank you.

HEALEY: Thanks for having me.

MELBER: Appreciate it.

END

data.title: Meridian Energy's Retail Sales Volumes Down 3% in March
data.description:
Meridian Energy's retail sales volumes declined 3.1% year on year to 786 gigawatt hours (GWh) in March from 811 GWh a year earlier, according to a Wednesday filing with the Australian and New Zealand bourses.

All segments recorded an increase, except for agriculture, where sales plunged nearly 41%.

National hydro storage increased to 77% from 76% of the historical average.

In the fiscal third quarter, retail sales volumes fell 4% on the year-ago period.

Inflows in the three months ended March were a record third-quarter low across 92 years of historical inflows, per the filing.

Meridian Energy's Kiwi shares were down almost 1% in recent Wednesday trade.

data.title: South Korea, Vietnam foreign ministers pledge cooperation over US tariffs
data.description:
The foreign ministers of South Korea and Vietnam agreed to cooperate closely in response to U.S. reciprocal tariffs imposed on the countries during a meeting in Hanoi, South Korea's foreign ministry said on Wednesday.

South Korean businesses, which are the largest foreign investors in Vietnam, are bracing for the impact of a 46% reciprocal tariff announced by U.S. President Donald Trump on imports from the Southeast Asian country.

South Korea is subject to a 25% duty under the sweeping trade plan by Trump against dozens of countries, which he has for now put on a 90-day moratorium until July.

Foreign Minister Cho Tae-yul met his Vietnamese counterpart Bui Thanh Son on Tuesday, the South Korean ministry said.

Earlier this week, South Korea's industry and trade minister also visited Vietnam to discuss the response to looming tariffs.

data.title: Japan Manufacturers’ Mood Hits 8-Month High, Outlook Worsens
data.description:
The Reuters Tankan sentiment index for manufacturers in Japan jumped to +9 in April 2025 from -1 in March, marking the highest reading since August last year.

Despite the upbeat current sentiment, the outlook for the next three months has darkened due to escalating concerns over U.S. trade policy.

The index is projected to fall back to zero as Japan braces for a 10% tariff on its exports to the U.S. and a steep 25% duty on automobiles.

Export-driven industries, particularly automotive and machinery, are sounding the alarm over declining orders and increased client caution.

Uncertainty surrounding U.S. tariffs remains the dominant concern, though some companies also pointed to weakening demand from China and the impact of low-cost Chinese goods undercutting domestic sales.

data.title: Ampol Expects 2Q Refiner Margins at March, April Lows>ALD.AU
Australia's Ampol posts 5.7% drop in first-quarter Lytton refinery output
data.description:
Australia's Ampol reported a 5.7% drop in first-quarter output from its Lytton refinery in Queensland on Wednesday, as a few days of production was lost to prepare for Cyclone Alfred to make landfall.

The company's refinery production was 1.30 billion litres for the first quarter, lower than the 1.38 billion litres produced in the same period last year.

data.title: Nvidia Chip Restrictions Likely Part of 'More Comprehensive Approach' by U.S.Market Talk
data.description: The Trump Administration's restrictions on Nvidia's exports of AI H20 chips to China looks to be part of a more comprehensive approach that we will learn about from the government soon, DA Davidson analyst Gil Luria tells The Wall Street Journal. Chip makers Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, Broadcom, Intel and Qualcomm down between about 2% to 4% in post-market trading. "The administration is focused on national security and it's a matter on how much is sold to China," Luria says. "The Chinese government is trying to beat the U.S. government and Chinese companies are trying to beat American companies." Nvidia would rather deal with tariffs than selling restrictions, Luria says, adding that around 20% to 30% of Nvidia's sales come from China or Chinese companies. Nvidia down 5.6% after-hours to $105.90. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

data.title: Ampol Expects Lower Fuel Prices to Be Tailwind for Retail Volumes>ALD.AU
Trump Seeks Ideas to Reduce Prescription Drug Costs
data.description:
By Janet H. Cho

The Trump administration announced that it is evaluating ways to reduce healthcare costs and lower the price of prescription drugs such as insulin and epinephrine pens.

In an executive order that was light on details, President Donald Trump directed federal health agencies to make recommendations and suggest legislation where needed to promote greater competition and a more efficient pharmaceutical value chain "that delivers lower prices for Americans."

One of the main initiatives directs agencies to work with Congress on revising a law that allows Medicare to negotiate prescription drug prices. Right now there is a difference between the timeline of eligibility for negotiation for common drugs known as "small molecule" drugs and more complex biologic drugs called "large molecule." The more complicated drugs get a longer window before they are eligible for negotiation.

The order also calls on officials including Health and Human Services Secretary Robert Kennedy Jr., Office of Management and Budget Director Russell Vought, the assistant to the president for Domestic Policy, and the assistant to the president for Economic Policy to develop ways to make insulin and injectable epinephrine available at lower cost to uninsured or low-income Americans.

Trump urged healthcare administrators to re-evaluate the role of "middlemen" such as pharmacy benefit managers. He wants to increase competition for high-cost drugs by accelerating the approval of generic and biosimilar drugs, combination products, and "second-in-class brand name medications," and by identifying prescription drugs that can be reclassified as over-the-counter medications.

White House officials said that recent reductions in force at Health and Human Services Departments won't affect the rollout of the initiatives. Food and Drug Administration inspections and reviews weren't impacted by those cuts, and the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services and other HHS divisions are "adequately staffed to be able to accomplish these goals," they said during a background briefing with reporters.

Trump also urged Kennedy to work with the Commissioner of Food and Drugs to streamline the importation program of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act to make it easier for states to import drugs from places like Canada "without sacrificing safety and quality."

White House officials skirted a question about how the import initiative would work given Trump's tariffs, which are currently in force on goods imported from Canada and are expected to be applied to pharmaceutical imports in general.

Write to Janet H. Cho at janet.cho@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Rio Tinto Expects 2025 Iron Ore Shipments at Low End of Guidance
data.description:
By Rhiannon Hoyle

Rio Tinto said iron ore shipments from its Australian mining operations are likely to be at the lower end of guidance following disruptions from four cyclones during the first quarter of the year.

The world's second-biggest miner by market value said it shipped 70.7 million metric tons of the steel ingredient from pits in Australia's minerals-rich Pilbara region in the three months through March.

That was 9% lower than the year-prior period and 17% below shipments in the quarter immediately prior.

Rio Tinto had projected it would ship between 323 million and 338 million tons from the operations in 2025.

The company last month estimated it lost about 13 million tons in shipments due to tropical cyclones Tahlia, Vince, Zelia and Sean.

"Mitigation plans are in place to offset around half of this and will require an additional investment of around A$150 million [US$95 million] for rectification works and contracting mining activities," the company said. "Lower volumes and recovery costs will be offset by a weaker than expected Australian dollar."

Write to Rhiannon Hoyle at rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com

data.title: Bank of Queensland Posts Higher Fiscal H1 Cash Earnings, Revenue
data.description:
Bank of Queensland reported Wednesday that its cash earnings per diluted share in the fiscal first half rose to AU$0.264 from AU$0.239 per diluted share a year earlier.

Analysts polled by Visible Alpha were expecting EPS of AU$0.20.

On a statutory basis, diluted EPS was AU$0.248, compared with AU$0.213 a year earlier.

Revenue from ordinary activities for the six months ended Feb. 28 was AU$802 million, up from AU$796 million in the same period a year earlier.

Analysts surveyed by Visible Alpha expected a total revenue of AU$805.4 million.

The board declared an interim dividend of AU$0.18 per share, up 6% from AU$0.17 in the previously reported period, payable on May 23 to shareholders on record as of May 1.
